g.data-narration: Welcome to Trees in Data Structures - a fundamental concept for computer science students.
Trees are non-linear hierarchical data structures with branching relationships between nodes, unlike linear arrays.
g.text: A non-linear hierarchical data structure consisting of
nodes connected by edges, with exactly one root and no cycles.
Unlike linear structures (arrays, lists), trees represent hierarchical relationships.

g.data-narration: Trees are essential for file systems, databases, and decision making - enabling efficient search and organization.
g.text: Tree: Hierarchical - XML, JSON, Org charts
Efficiency: O(log n) search vs O(n) linear

g.data-narration: Key tree terms: root (top node), parent (node's owner), child (node's descendant), leaf (no children).
g.text: Depth: Edges from root to node

g.data-narration: Binary trees restrict each node to maximum two children: left child and right child, enabling simpler algorithms.
g.text: A binary tree is a tree where each node has at most two children.
Children are specifically called left child and right child.
This restriction simplifies implementation and enables efficient algorithms for search, sort, and traversal.

g.data-narration: Three important binary tree types: complete (left-filled), perfect (all levels full), full (0 or 2 children).
Binary Search Tree: left subtree has smaller values, right subtree has larger values - enables O(log n) search.
g.text: Binary Search Tree (BST) - The Key Property
For every node: Left subtree values < Node value < Right subtree values

g.data-narration: Step-by-step BST insertion: 50→30→70→20→40→60→80. Each value goes left if smaller, right if larger.
Inorder traversal: Left-Root-Right. For BST, this always produces sorted output - remember this key property!
Preorder: Root first, then Left subtree, then Right subtree. Useful for copying trees and prefix expressions.
Postorder: Left, Right, Root - visits root LAST. Useful for deleting trees and evaluating expression trees.
Tree height is edges on longest path from root to leaf. Use recursion: height equals one plus maximum of subtrees.
